title: Revealing Hidden Correlations in a Fermi-Hubbard system via Interaction Ramps
authors: Botond Oreg, Carter Turnbaugh, Jens Hertkorn, Ningyuan Jia, Martin Zwierlein
abstract: We observe an enhanced visibility of charge-density-wave correlations in a cold-atom realization of the attractive Hubbard model following a rapid boost of the interaction strength. The interaction boost associates nonlocal pairs into doublons which mark the center of mass of the original pairs. The enhancement is largest in the strongly correlated regime where pairing is nonlocal. We distinguish the unpaired Fermi liquid from the pseudogap phase of preformed pairs by analyzing atom-resolved spin-charge correlations after the ramp. The technique we establish here may facilitate the observation of exotic forms of pair order in spin-imbalanced systems, and of stripe order in the dual case of the doped repulsive Hubbard model.
summary: The researchers developed a technique using a rapid interaction ramp to make nonlocal pairs visible as local doublons in a 2D Fermi-Hubbard system. This method successfully reveals hidden charge-density-wave correlations and distinguishes between the Fermi liquid and pseudogap regimes. It offers a new way to study complex phases like stripe order or FFLO states in ultracold atoms.
main_problem: Detecting hidden spatial correlations, specifically charge-density-wave order, in the strongly interacting regime of the attractive Hubbard model where nonlocal pairs are difficult to image.
main_result: An interaction ramp technique significantly enhances the visibility of charge-density-wave correlations by projecting nonlocal pairs into detectable local doublons. This allows for the clear distinction between the Fermi liquid and the pseudogap phase.
method: A rapid linear magnetic field ramp (1 ms) is used to increase the interaction strength, contracting extended pairs into local doublons before single-site, spin-resolved fluorescence imaging.
model_or_system: A 2D spin-balanced Fermi gas of 40K atoms in a square optical lattice, realizing the attractive Hubbard model with controlled tunneling (t) and interaction (U) via Feshbach resonance.
key_observables: Doublon density, density-density correlators, density structure factor, charge-density-wave strength, singlon fraction, and spin/magnetization correlations.
important_parameters: Interaction strength U/t (ranging from weak/Fermi liquid to strong/local pairs), tunneling t = 340 Hz, temperature T/t ~ 0.5, and lattice depth.
main_assumptions: The ramp is fast enough to prevent many-body evolution but slow enough to allow pair contraction; the density distribution remains frozen during the ramp.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the ramp schematic and enhanced CDW patterns; Fig 2 displays density-density correlation maps; Fig 4 compares magnetization fluctuations and singlon density across different U/t regimes.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of hidden correlations, describes the experimental setup and the interaction ramp technique, presents results showing enhanced CDW visibility and the identification of the pseudogap phase, and concludes with potential applications to spin-imbalanced and repulsive Hubbard models.
why_interesting: This provides a powerful new experimental tool for observing exotic pair orders and phase transitions in many-body systems by using non-equilibrium dynamics to reveal static correlations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Rapid ramp in an attractive Fermi-Hubbard gas. (a) The attraction strength between fermions can be dynamically tuned near a Feshbach resonance. A rapid ramp of the interaction strength associates nonlocal pairs into local doublons. This reveals otherwise hidden order between pairs, e.g. a charge-density-wave pattern. (b) Two experimental realizations of the same, strongly correlated, Fermi-Hubbard system (U/t = 5.8(3)) without (left) and with (right) the rapid ramp applied.

FIG. 2. Revealing charge-density-wave (CDW) corre- lations with the rapid ramp. (a) Density-density corre- lation maps as a function of interaction strength U/t without (upper) and with (lower) the rapid ramp. (b) Fourier trans- forms of the density correlation maps at various interaction strengths along the trace (0, 0) →(π, 0) →(π, π) →(0, 0). A peak at (π, π) indicates CDW structure.
